speaker: interviewee
text: Tape three, Lily [? Frank ?] Grossman. Continue your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was Auschwitz, we arrived. We-- we're looking for each other, who came with us and who don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know yet, again, what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any idea?

speaker: interviewee
text: They said the grandmothers is going to be with the little children. Let them to go together, and tomorrow you are going to meet them. They'd calm us down. So we went to a building for shower. But first day, all over our body was hair cutting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shaving.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shaving, whatever. But it was stinks everywhere, terrible smell. You know? They-- they used some liquid to [? helping ?] or--

speaker: interviewer
text: An antiseptic, you think, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Antiseptic. Something like that, yeah. But we didn't know that. And they let us to put on our shoes, dress, everything in one pile. And we said, OK. The third window under our pile. Mother put together. Make one pile for us. So we did. Very careful.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went in the shower. And it was shower. Otherwise, the most unlucky people, they get gas from it, in the gas chamber from the shower.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we got shower. We were chose for work. Everybody, from 14 years to 50 or 55, able to work, people was coming with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the shower was all wet. No soap. No towel. Nothing. We just walked.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all over among us was German soldier with the German Shepherd dog. If somebody started to talk or cry, the German Shepherd dog was jumping on their shoulder and get on the person and between the-- the teeth, hold the throat of the person. Just to see that. Not to be there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw it yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, I saw. So nobody should say a single word. We were happy to keep quiet.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about someone who panicked? Were there a lot of people-- did you see people panicking at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They beat up. They beat up. And the dog was called again. They told the dog, Jude, which means Jewish, the dog right away was in action. Right away. Just one word. Nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went further. And in the next room, or big place, something, it was thrown one piece of clothes. I got a-- one piece clothes. Nothing. No underwear. Nothing. And a pair of shoes with a wooden sole and material. Not leather. Canvas. Like canvas. Canvas?

speaker: interviewee
text: Canvas, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was it. Out, out, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And we came out. And with shaven head we hardly could recognize each other. We were looking to each other. Are you really, my mother asked. You know what I was looking like, exactly like my younger brother, [? Tibika. ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She thought it is [? Tibika ?] there. So we were again to force, force with the dogs, with the rubber stick, everything, in one group. Big group. You couldn't see the front, and you couldn't see the end. I can't tell you how many people. It was a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were started walking on stones, sharp stones. So we went-- we still saw-- we still saw the [INAUDIBLE] And we saw barracks and fence. And we were taken for the [? lively ?] fence, bushes.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the gas chamber. But we didn't know what is that. We thought it is-- this is the kitchen with the chimney.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were walking and walking. Finally we arrived. A very primitive, just did not finished camp that was B3. B3. And we were taken in the third barrack, number three barrack. And between number two and three was a little-- in-- a little-- water was-- Like a stream? A little bit of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Stream.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a natural--

speaker: interviewee
text: More-- more in stream. Very, very-- and it was hot. So it was very low. That was a very big things in our life later on, because we went on our stomach to drink. Five meter from us people was washing something. And some other people was washing their face and was drinking. We didn't see any--

speaker: interviewer
text: Water.

speaker: interviewee
text: --water, ever. So we were taken in the barrack. And they said, lie down. Which means like sardines.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the barrack empty? Was anybody occupying--

speaker: interviewee
text: Empty--

speaker: interviewer
text: --it before you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only wood and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And no other people that had been there from before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, we were the new people over there. When one and two was full, the third one was used. And that was ours.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went in the barrack and lied down on the bare wooden. Nothing on. And I was watching in the window myself, how do I look like? And I was looking backward, because I thought my [? Tibika, ?] my brother is standing behind me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I ran to my mother, Mother, I am [INAUDIBLE], your daughter. It was terrible. We were hungry. We were thirsty. We were tired.

speaker: interviewer
text: We didn't have our own [? soul. ?] I'm telling you. We were already like animal. I am-- I am sure. And what they think? Who is going to give us to eat? And where is the place we are going to sleep? So we were happy just to lay down. And we have very close two friends and one cousin. So we were there five. Five means in our life until we were in [? deportant ?] people, five in a line.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we keep together. We came home together. We went to work together. We did everything together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we lied down. But next day, we were smarter. We went out, and we finally saw a little grass. So we were getting grass to get in the barrack and put it under our head. You pulled up enough grass to--

speaker: interviewee
text: A little grass. Not too much. It wasn't too much. Grass, because our fresh shaven head was hurting us. You know, because on the wooden--

speaker: interviewer
text: Boards.

speaker: interviewee
text: --board. Yeah. Board. It was-- everywhere it was sand in the whole Auschwitz. We brought in on our shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they start at 4 o'clock, 5 o'clock, I think, at the morning. Up! Up! Up! Zahlappell. Who knows what is Zahlappell? So we went out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the [? block over, ?] two, three, Polish girl has their room in the first place of the barrack. Two rooms they have for them, for the kapos. We didn't know they are kapos. Girl, Polish girl. And they started to screaming, Keep quiet! Your parents is goes in the smoke. We are six years over here. We built that place for you. You were dancing in Budapest, music, tango. We were already suffering here. We didn't know, again, what they are talking about. They hate us. We hate them. So we went out. And they wanted us like-- like soldier to keep the five people in lane.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a row. When-- when they said that your parents or whatever went up, did you know then what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You still didn't know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing. Why should they? They said we are going to meet. The grandmother is going to be with the children, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: But when these girls said that it's going up in smoke?

speaker: interviewee
text: Still didn't make any-- not next day. Not next month. OK? Never, ever. We realized what they are talking about, or it is a gas chamber who-- where they killed millions of people. We didn't know. Don't say I am stupid. Nobody knows.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody told you? The other prisoners never said anything to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't see other prisoners. We were just Hungarian people this time there. Whoever came to the lager, to B, our lager section, from the C lager, because it was alphabetically, they didn't talk. They didn't say anything. We didn't know is existing, a gas chamber. Nothing. Don't tell me ever anybody knows. And Zahlappell.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, when-- OK. Sorry. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that keeps us [INAUDIBLE] until they contact the whole lager, not just our barrack, everyone. And [SPEAKING GERMAN] until they didn't give up the very precious counting and everybody gave [INAUDIBLE] us, up to 8 o'clock, three, four hours. And it was later cold nights, cold early mornings. And we just get together like lambs. We were frozen with one piece of clothes on us. But later, the Jewish brain, we get to help ourself. We got very few food, first of all. Our line, the five people, we got one bowl of soup at night and cheese. But I think that cheese, they said this is a very good cheese. And-- and I think it was like crawling cheese worms in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Doesn't matter. We ate. OK? That doesn't matter. At morning we got a bowl of black coffee. Chicory or something. Chicory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a coffee. Chicory, yeah. And one piece of bread. And one little piece of marmalade, or one little piece of jam, either way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we didn't know if we eat the bread at morning, because we were very hungry from a day, a day, a day before, or keep the bread. And we put under our dress, because if you left here, they steal. They steal. That's it. That's the truth. So that was Auschwitz. And they took us for selection, again, which means we went in a big place and empty. And we kept in our hand the shoes and other hand dress up. And naked we went around, around, around. So that was-- it was Mengele again, or somebody else. I'm not sure. They selected for work. So some people went. But that was the trouble with the bread. Should we take the bread or quickly eat? What happens, it happens. Or-- or hide away. So some of us hide away. Some of us eat. I would eat my portion. And we kept my mother's portion hiding. So we came back. Oh, you are back. You are back also. So that's [INAUDIBLE]. Some questions about some of the girls that-- that you were with. What was their behavior like during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. We were sitting outside in the-- in the dust.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were cooking. We gave recipe to each other, dreaming about food, or nothing. And one day-- I was lucky, because I was always in my life a very hard-working girl, OK? And there, I, every morning, we can [INAUDIBLE] we were shaking out the [GERMAN], the kapos, the [GERMAN] feather decks and blankets.

speaker: interviewer
text: So therefore, I don't remember. I got one or two blankets for-- from them, because I have to take care of my mother. Sure

speaker: interviewee
text: Right? So we have a-- to cover ourself, the five girls. And people was hating me and us, because we had feather deck, or blanket to cover. They hate us.

speaker: interviewer
text: For sure. Did they try to take it from you?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I don't know why they-- I offered. I want to make these for you, the kapos. But I didn't know that time what kapo means. They were the [GERMAN], [? Luba ?] and [? Genya. ?] I didn't know their power or their job or anything. They-- they said what they-- what the German told them.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were not afraid of them, so you approached them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't afraid because I always believed-- and that saved my life later on. So one day, they wanted 20 girls from the barrack, the block, to go somewhere, to bring back something, somewhere something. They didn't tell us what.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all of the [INAUDIBLE] the 20 girls from the barrack. And I was taken also. But that could be wrong also. Never come back. But they took a chance. Took a chance. They told me to go. So we went to a place. I don't know what they call German, where they kept a-- like a warehouse. They kept dressers and many, many things. So we have to bring back to the block those things.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we all came back like we are nine months pregnant, because we steal right away and put on the-- first of all, we wanted right away something that we can put on our waist. And then we made up ourself like that and our stomach like that. So we came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I, the [? stupid, ?] wanted a Siddur, a prayer book. I said, don't you have a prayer book? Because it was many things all over in there. And the-- the Polish Jewish people who were working over there, we want to [NON-ENGLISH]-- that was always the word, compliment-- said, the [NON-ENGLISH]. You want to daven? You want to pray? For whom? The god who send you here? But I said I would like to have one. So they gave me one. Next day, so we came home. We give our package-- I think, if I am correct, we were pushing something what we pegged up many things on it, dressers with four wheels, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I tell you, every one of us was like pregnant. So we came. And that was unbelievable, life saving, better than any life insurance.

speaker: interviewer
text: So next day, two Polish guy from this place came. And they wanted to speak with the girl who needs prayer book. Not-- I thought, finito. And they started again. Whom you want to say, are you going to be the [INAUDIBLE] here? Pray whom? Who send you here? So it wasn't-- didn't happen anything. But that's was the end of the-- Why did they want to speak to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ask them. That was the end of the rope-- could be. But I survived again. So one of our girl find a piece of [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Tin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tin. Thank you. And from the top of the Margarine box she was making two stick for make knitting. You know what I mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: She made herself knitting needles?

speaker: interviewee
text: From tin.

speaker: interviewer
text: She cut the-- she cut the plastic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She made stick, two and other two. And I brought a sweater under my dress. So my mother made a thread from each.

speaker: interviewer
text: She unraveled it. Opened up the-- the wool.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And she made all of us socks. Life saving. Not [INAUDIBLE] or-- or Bloomingdale could make better things than that was, OK? But we have to hide it also. We never-- have either on our feet or in the dress. You never could put it down. Never. But that morning, we were saved in the cold, because we had-- and, you know, one day she could make three pairs. So all of our four of us had. And my mother made some underwear from another piece. That went-- that was life. Finally, end of July we went again for selection. Again, shoes, dress, naked, walking, German dog Shepherd was there. The Germans, doesn't matter. We were walking. And we were taken to-- we didn't go back to the barrack anymore. And who was selected or separated from mother, from sister, it was final. There's no asking or anything. Finally, our five people we went together. This time, my mother put on a white kerchief, because we have a piece of-- piece of [INAUDIBLE] already. And I put on black. So the age difference we wanted to eliminate.

speaker: interviewer
text: So always my mother went first. Then I could follow her. And that was terrible, because we didn't know if they let us together or not. So our five people stayed together. You're very lucky.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went for a shower again. And we got coat. Coat.

speaker: interviewer
text: They gave you coats.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Luckily, I get a small one. But in Hungarian Europe men put on, when they started to puffing cigar, they call it cigar coat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Smoking jacket.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Smoking jacket. Lucky me. Other people got a rabbi's shiny--

speaker: interviewer
text: Kapata.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kapata, with the things, you know? The other people get a wool coat or whatever. We look like-- like we came out from the comic section. But we had kerchief this time already. And we have more clothes, because we always requested somehow stealing. So we were sitting outside night and day in the cold and in-- under the sun.

speaker: interviewer
text: So next day, they put us in the-- our train came. And we were put on the train. And we went beautiful places. I think it was something, Magdeburg-- Magburg-- Magdeburg, or something. It was like in a-- the Hanukkah cookie houses. Gingerbread houses.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gingerbread houses in-- in existing. And children was playing and playing ball and everything. And we just watched out. And we didn't know it is a world like that is existing anymore. We didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this? Do you have a date?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was end of July.

speaker: interviewer
text: End of-- so how long were you in Auschwitz before they transported you?

speaker: interviewee
text: From-- about five, six weeks. No more.

speaker: interviewer
text: No more.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in this time, one day it could be the very end of July or very first of August. We don't have--

speaker: interviewer
text: The date.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anything, you know. We saw the moon. And when the moon was new, we knew it is [INAUDIBLE] is a new month came. And that was it. No time. No nothing-- no calendar. Nothing. And we got lots of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother manage to light those candles that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Never?

speaker: interviewee
text: She left already in the ghetto, in the-- in the steel factory-- I mean, in the brick factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was there any observance at all of any kind during the time that you were in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. When I have the prayer book, I stand up. And I was davening for my little group. And that's it. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get a tattoo when you were in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They didn't gave this time. It was too late.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, by then it was too late.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, the Russian front, they came already backward, backward. And everybody knows. We don't. But everybody else knows if it is the end of the war is coming. So they didn't bother with number to us. We just was there. That's it. And then the late July night somewhere, the gypsy lager was evacuated. And that was my brother. This time we know, because next day we were walking over there beside. And it was quiet. Not a single person was [INAUDIBLE]. But the gypsies, they know what's happening. They knew. They are going to be taken to the gas chamber. They knew everything. They were close to it, and they learn. They were there years. But so is the all 14, 16-years-old boy went with them this night. They were noisy. Screaming.

speaker: interviewer
text: You heard it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Screaming-- what?

speaker: interviewer
text: You heard? Did you hear something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. We were sitting up and praying and thinking about. And that was so noisy. And they were afraid all the lager-- lager is going to [? rioting. ?] They brought a band. It was a beautiful, like Philharmonic band. Not one, two. And they were playing. They overpowered the screaming of the gypsies.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was beautiful music, better than Mozart, everything. From the whole of Europe the best musician was there. Sorry. May I have a little water? [DRINKS] So we went to Stuthoff by the train. We didn't know either. But was very nice. The-- if you wanted to see the scenery, it was very nice, we arrived in Stuthoff.

speaker: interviewee
text: And again, five line up. Five in a row. So the-- we were somehow separated, not from my mother, from my group, not the five people. And we were the second row in a new--

speaker: interviewer
text: Group.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hundred people. We were crying. No. We are separated. What's going to happen with us? But that was a good luck. [INAUDIBLE] If you could call it good luck, because we went to our room. From there, the [INAUDIBLE] people was taken out to Zahappell, to counting. And they never let them to go back. And we find everything. We find food. We find bowl. We find [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Spoon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Spoon. [LAUGHS] Yeah. You know. The word is get out because I am there now. Prayer book. Sweater. Socks that was [INAUDIBLE], that was better than anything you could--

speaker: interviewer
text: Valuables. The most valuable thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Value everythings. So we were so rich. And because we were in the first 10, the second, we were-- we went in. So we chose our own bed. It was bed there. You know, just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Real-- yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three or four-- three bed, I think, on each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. we're gonna stop.
